coredata.dc:title: Polybromopyrrole-derived nitrogen-containing polymer dots: Synthesis, optical properties, and insight into their fluorescence quenching by aromatic compounds
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zhenzhen
Gai, Ligang
Zhou, Jianhua
Jiang, Haihui
Tian, Yan
coredata.dc:description: A simple approach has been designed for the synthesis of nitrogen-containing carbogenic polymer dots (NPDs) with relatively high nitrogen concentration (10.5at.%), tunable size distribution, high yield (25%), and a high degree of graphitization, through a microwave-assisted polymerization of polybromopyrroles in an alkaline ethanolthermal environment at 80°C. The structure, composition, morphology, and optical properties of NPDs were characterized with X-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, ζ-potential, transmission electron microscopy, scanning probe microscopy, UV–vis absorbance, and photoluminescence techniques. At excitation wavelengths (λ ex ) before and after 360nm, NPDs exhibit a dual emission behavior in terms of λ ex -independence and λ ex -dependence, respectively. The absolute quantum yield of NPDs is greatly improved to be 24% from 3% after simple surface reduction by NaBH4. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of NPDs has been carefully investigated by using twenty aromatic compounds as quenchers. Through theoretical calculations and instrumental analysis, we conclude that non-photochemical quenching rather than the fluorescence resonance energy transfer and charge transfer dominates the quenching of NPDs by aromatic compounds. This insight shows the way for selective fluorescent detection of trace aromatic compounds with carbogenic dot-based sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-containing polymer dots
Fluorescence quenching
Fluorescent detection
Aromatic compounds

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of NPDs by ACs
Fluorescence quenching of NPDs by ACs
Selective fluorescent detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol with NPDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 51272143 and 51472278 . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the formation of NPDs with N,O-containing functional groups. For simplicity, only the attack of HO- on the edge of the polymer is presented.
(a) FT-IR spectrum; (b) 13C NMR spectrum; (c) C 1s; (d) N 1s; (e) O 1s; (f) ζ-potential plot of NPDs in water.
(a) 2D-SPM image; (b) 3D-SPM image; (c) depth histogram corresponding to a; (d) TEM image; (e) high-resolution TEM image; inset in d is the particle size distribution fitted with Log-Normal function.

(a) UV–vis spectra and PLE spectra (inset); (b) emission spectra as a function of λex and normalized PL spectra (inset); (c) emission spectra as a function of pH; (d) steady fluorescence decay curves of NPDs and r-NPDs; (e) emission spectra of r-NPDs with different NaBH4 concentrations; (f) λex-dependent emissions of r-NPDs.
Diagrams of the HOMO and LUMO levels of 20 ACs and NPDs.
Plots of quenching degree of NPDs as a function of λex; inset is the corresponding UV–vis absorbance spectra.

(a) Emission spectra of NPDs in aqueous solutions containing different concentrations of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol; inset is the plot of I 0/I versus quencher concentration; (b) emission spectra of NPDs with and without ACs; all the emission spectra are excited at 360nm.

head.ce:title: Polybromopyrrole-derived nitrogen-containing polymer dots: Synthesis, optical properties, and insight into their fluorescence quenching by aromatic compounds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology, Jinan 250353, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple approach has been designed for the synthesis of nitrogen-containing carbogenic polymer dots (NPDs) with relatively high nitrogen concentration (10.5at.%), tunable size distribution, high yield (25%), and a high degree of graphitization, through a microwave-assisted polymerization of polybromopyrroles in an alkaline ethanolthermal environment at 80°C. The structure, composition, morphology, and optical properties of NPDs were characterized with X-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, ζ-potential, transmission electron microscopy, scanning probe microscopy, UV–vis absorbance, and photoluminescence techniques. At excitation wavelengths (λ ex ) before and after 360nm, NPDs exhibit a dual emission behavior in terms of λ ex -independence and λ ex -dependence, respectively. The absolute quantum yield of NPDs is greatly improved to be 24% from 3% after simple surface reduction by NaBH4. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of NPDs has been carefully investigated by using twenty aromatic compounds as quenchers. Through theoretical calculations and instrumental analysis, we conclude that non-photochemical quenching rather than the fluorescence resonance energy transfer and charge transfer dominates the quenching of NPDs by aromatic compounds. This insight shows the way for selective fluorescent detection of trace aromatic compounds with carbogenic dot-based sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the accidental discovery of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles during the separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [1], tremendous interest has been triggered in search for this new type of carbogenic nanomaterials featured with bright luminescence, excellent water solubility, facile functionalization, low toxicity, and high physicochemical and photochemical stability [2,3]. Carbogenic nanoparticles with size smaller than 10nm are referred to as carbogenic quantum dots, carbogenic nanodots, or carbogenic dots (CDs) [4]. According to the reports in literature, CDs can be tentatively classified into carbon dots [1,3–24], graphene dots [25–31], carbon nitride dots [32], and polymer dots [33,34]. So far, many approaches have been developed for the synthesis of CDs such as pyrolysis [4,18], laser ablation [24], ultrasonication [14], microwave irradiation [4,26], microemulsion [19,23], chemical oxidation [12], electrochemical etching [10], and solvothermal/hydrothermal synthesis [4,13,15]. Among the approaches, microwave-assisted solvothermal/hydrothermal synthesis is considered an effective route to achieve high-quality luminescent CDs [3,26]. This is because microwave irradiation provides rapid and uniform heating to the reaction medium, thereby facilitating to shorten the reaction time and to improve the purity of the product [3].

As demonstrated in literature [24–26], the photoluminescence (PL) behavior of CDs is highly dependent on their surface functional groups. In the case of amino-functionalized carbogenic dots (NCDs), their optoelectronic, electrocatalytic, and chemical sensing properties are also distinctive from those of CDs without nitrogen [18–22,25]. However, the synthesis of NCDs in previous reports usually involves relatively high-temperature pyrolysis/hydrothermal treatment [18–20], low nitrogen concentration [19], and/or complicated procedures [21]. This makes it highly desirous for simple synthesis of NCDs with relatively high nitrogen concentration.

As raw materials, aromatic compounds (ACs) in particular nitroaromatic compounds (NACs) are widely used in chemical synthesis of pesticides, dyes, and explosives [15,22,28,35,36]. Their emissions into the environment pose serious health problems such as hepatitis, cancer, neurodegenerative Alzheimer’s disease, and Parkinson’s disease etc. [36]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop simple, sensitive, and reliable methods for rapid and effective detection of NACs [35,36]. Traditional instrumental analysis for qualitative and quantitative detection of NACs involves expensive apparatuses and complex pretreatment of samples [35]. In contrast, nanomaterial-based sensors offer low cost, portable, specific, and rapid identification of trace NACs [35]. Since the pioneering research reported on luminescent response of PEG1500N-passivated carbon dots to 4-nitrotoluene, 2,4-dinitrotoluene, and N,N-diethylaniline in 2009 [12], fluorescent detection of ACs with CDs has attracted much attention due to the rapid luminescent response, low toxicity, non-photobleaching, and low cost of the dots [15,20,22,28,29,35]. Through instrumental analysis such as UV–vis absorbance and time-resolved single-photon counting techniques, four types of fluorescence quenching mechanisms relative to CDs have been suggested, i.e. fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [15,28,29], charge transfer [5,29,30], exciplex/Meisenheimer complex formation [15], and electrostatic interaction [22]. Although the aforementioned mechanisms seem fit for explaining some specific experimental phenomena [15,22,28,29], there is still lack of insight into the origin of fluorescence quenching of CDs by ACs. This is because both the FRET and the charge transfer mechanisms are dependent on whether or not the energy levels of quenchers match well with those of CDs [5,30]. However, there are no data documented in previous reports with respect to the energy levels of ACs and CDs, making it questionable for assigning the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs with ACs to FRET and/or charge transfer [15,28,29].

In this paper, we provide a simple synthesis of nitrogen-containing carbogenic polymer dots (NPDs) through a microwave-assisted ethanolthermal approach, using polybromopyrroles (PBPs) as the starting material. The synthetic strategy is illustrated in Fig. 1 (discussion below). The selection of PBPs as the starting material is based on the facts that (i) polyhalogenated pyrroles are instable at room temperature [37] and (ii) high nitrogen concentration (20at.%) exists in the idealized polymer of PBPs [38]. Thus, an idea is inspired to produce NPDs with high nitrogen concentration at a relatively low temperature. To clarify the origin of fluorescence quenching of NPDs by ACs, theoretical calculations in combination with instrumental analysis were performed to acquire the energy levels of NPDs and quenchers, which consist of 20 ACs. The results show that non-photochemical quenching rather than the FRET and charge transfer dominates the fluorescence quenching of NPDs by ACs. This paper provides new insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs with ACs, paving the way for selective fluorescent detection of trace ACs with CD-based sensors.

A microwave-assisted ethanolthermal approach was adopted to synthesize NPDs at a relatively low temperature of 80°C. Briefly, 5mL of newly-formed PBP ethanol solution (0.25molL−1) was added into a flask containing 40mL of ethanol, followed by addition of 1.3g of KOH. The concentration of PBPs in ethanol is ca. 28mmolL−1 (28mM). After agitation at room temperature for 15min, the mixture was subjected to microwave-assisted heat treatment at 80°C for 40min. The microwave power was set at 200W. After removal of the solid inorganic salts, the claret solution was transferred into dialysis bags (MWCO 300, USA). To obtain solid polymer dots, the dialyzed solution was subjected to freeze-drying. The yield of NPDs was calculated to be ca. 25% according to the amount of PBPs in solution, much higher than that of 7.8% for preparing nitrogen-containing carbon dots through hydrothermal treatment of chitosan [19]. The synthesis of PBPs was published in our previous report [38] and provided in Supplementary data (S1). In control experiments, the concentration of PBPs in reaction medium was varied to be 50 and 83mM by keeping the molar ratio of PBPs/KOH constant. The corresponding NPD samples were named NPD-28, NPD-50, and NPD-83, respectively. For comparison, polymer nanoparticles were obtained by the microwave-assisted ethanolthermal approach without KOH, and named PPBP.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5406Å). Raman spectra were collected at room temperature on a Horiba Jobin Yvon laser confocal micro-Raman spectrometer with 532-nm yttrium aluminum garnet (YAG) laser. CHN elemental analysis was performed on an Elementar Analysensysteme Vario EL III element analyzer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a JEOL JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope, operating at an accelerating voltage of 150kV. Scanning probe microscopy (SPM) images were taken on a Multimode 8 Nanoscope V system scanning probe microscope. For SPM observation, sample aqueous solution was dip-coated onto a mica sheet and then dried. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected on a Shimadzu IRPrestige-21 infrared spectrometer using pressed KBr discs. The FT-IR spectra were recorded with resolution of 4cm−1 over the range of 4000–400cm−1. 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum was recorded on a Bruker Advance II 400 NMR spectrometer, using D2O as the solvent. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, using monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6eV). To record C1s, N1s, and O1s high-resolution spectra, pass energy of 50eV and step size of 0.05eV were adopted. Binding energies for the high-resolution spectra were calibrated by setting C1s at 284.8eV. ζ-Potential measurements were conducted on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 apparatus, after quantitative addition of NPD powders into pre-prepared solutions with constant ionic strength (0.01molkg−1 KCl) and designated pH (2–11).

The room-temperature PL spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-4500FL fluorescence spectrometer. Time-resolved fluorescence life time measurements were performed on an Edinburgh FLS 920 time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) spectrometer. The time-resolved emission spectra were recorded at excitation of 362nm. The fluorescence decay curves were fitted to multiexponential functions according to Eq. (1) [32]:
(1)
I
( t )
=
∑
⁣
B i
exp
(
− t
τ i
)
where I(t) is the intensity usually assumed to decay as the sum of individual single exponential decays, B i is the pre-exponential factor, and τi is the decay time.

The average life time (τ avg) was determined by Eq. (2) [32]:
(2)
τ avg
=
∑
i
B i
τ i 2
∑
i
B i
τ i

PL quantum yield (QY) measurements were carried out by using an integrating sphere (Labsphere) with 325-nm He–Cd laser (Kimmon Electric Co.). To obtain the QY, the excitation range and the luminescence range were set at 357–374.2nm and 374.2–650nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of NPDs by ACs
ce:section.ce:para:
Before fluorescence quenching tests, quantitative ACs were separately dissolved in ethanol, followed by addition of redistilled water to produce solutions with designated concentration through the volumetric method. During the fluorescence quenching tests, an aliquot of NPD aqueous solution was mixed with an aliquot of AC water/ethanol solution, followed by instantly measuring the PL emission spectra of the mixed solution with excitation wavelength (λ ex) of 360nm. In the case of selective fluorescent detection, an aliquot of NPD aqueous solution was mixed with several aliquots of solutions containing different ACs, respectively. The degree of fluorescence quenching (D FQ) was calculated by Eq. (3):
(3)
D FQ
=
I 0
− I
I 0
× 100 %
where I 0 and I represent the maximum PL emission intensity of NPD solution without and with ACs, respectively.

Theoretical calculations were performed to achieve the HOMO–LUMO energy levels of 20 ACs tested in the fluorescence quenching experiments. Gaussian 03 program package was used for the theoretical calculations through a B3LYP method at the 6-31G(d) basis sets, based on the density functional theory (DFT).

In a microwave-assisted solvothermal synthesis with KOH, PBPs condensate to form nanodots tethered by hydroxyls through cyclodebromination and nucleophilic substitution of HO− for Br− (Fig. 1a) [38], because halogenated pyrroles are highly reactive even at room temperature [37]. In this research, the high reactivity of PBPs was confirmed by the formation of PPBP at −18°C during the storage of PBPs within 7 days. With prolonging the reaction time, ether bond is formed through dehydration of two adjacent hydroxyls and amide groups appears due to electron transfer motivated by attack of HO− on the periphery. This results in ring opening of the polymer (Fig. 1b). Further attack of HO− on the polymer results in opening of the pyrrole ring, concomitant with the formation of primary amine and carboxyl (Fig. 1c and d). In brief, the nucleophilic substitution of HO− for Br− not only reduces the degree of polymerization of PPBP but also makes the polymer tethered by N,O-containing functional groups, leading to the formation of water soluble NPDs.

In the following sections, NPDs represent the NPD-28 sample unless otherwise specified because the three NPD samples resemble each other. The XRD pattern of NPDs shows a broad peak centered at 24° with d spacing of 0.37nm (Fig. 2 a), larger than that of 0.34nm for d 002 of graphite and close to the d 002 of carbon-based materials with turbostratic disorder [4,31]. This is attributed to the existence of surface functional groups at the edge and on the basal plane of sp2-hybridized CN x network cross-linked by sp3-hybridized NC bond [31,38]. The NPD Raman spectrum (Fig. 2b) exhibits two broad peaks centered at 1348 and 1566cm−1, which can be fitted into four bands (D4, D1, D3, and G+D2) through Lorentzian functions to reveal more structural information [38,39]. The bands at 1251 (D4, 10% in total peak areas) and 1337cm−1 (D1, 25.1%) are associated with the A1g breathing vibration phonon, which is activated by the neighboring sp3 defects [18]. The band at 1435cm−1 (D3, 28.4%) is assigned to amorphous carbon. The band at 1569cm−1 (G+D2, 36.5%) corresponds to the E2g vibration mode of the sp2-hybridized carbon in a two-dimentional hexagonal lattice [14,18]. As documented in literature [40], the intensity ratio of I D/I G is indicative of the degree of graphitization in partially graphitic carbons, and the I D/I G value decreases as the sp2 domain size increases. Here, we use I D1/I (G+D2) to reflect the degree of graphitization of NPDs, yielding a value of 0.71 which is much lower than that of nitrogen-containing CDs through hydrothermal treatment of chitosan [19]. This indicates a high degree of graphitization of the NPDs derived from self-propagated cyclodebromination of PBPs through the microwave-assisted ethanolthermal synthesis.

The surface chemistry of NPDs is revealed by the FT-IR and XPS techniques (Fig. 3 ). The peaks marked in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3a) correspond separately to the v(OH/NH) stretching vibration (3420cm−1), v as(CH)/v s(CH) asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations (2940 and 2870cm−1) in association with sp3-hybridized carbon atoms, v(CO) stretching vibration (1702cm−1), v as(CC)/v s(CC) ring-stretching vibrations (1650 and 1436cm−1), δ(OH) deforming vibrations (1382cm−1), v(CN) stretching vibration (1285cm−1), v(COH)/v(COC) stretching vibrations (1122, 1084, and 1039cm−1), and δ(CAr H)/γ(OH) deforming vibrations (1160, 878, and 746cm−1) [4,8,26,31]. The FT-IR results not only confirm the formation of sp2-hybridized CN x network cross-linked by sp3-hybridized NC bond [38] but also indicate ring opening of the polymer due to the occurrence of sp3-hybridized carbon atoms. The sp3- and sp2-hybridized carbon components are also confirmed by the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 3b), where signals in the range of 16–40, 50–85, 110–160, and 165–190ppm correspond separately to sp3-hybridized carbons (CC/CO/CN) and sp2-hybridized CC, CO, and HOCO/NCO [5,6,18,20,33].

The surface functional groups are further confirmed by the XPS spectra. Through Gaussian functions, the high-resolution C1s spectrum can be fitted into four bands centered at 284.2 (33.4% in total area), 285.5 (24.9%), 287.8 (10.2%), and 293.4eV (31.5%), corresponding separately to CC, CC/CN/CO, CO, and OCO components (Fig. 3c) [20,22,24,34]. Also, three nitrogen components in Fig. 3d signaling CNC, CNH and NCO are resolved at 398.8 (41.2%), 399.7 (42.4%), and 401.3eV (16.4%), respectively [18,20,22,34]. Using the C1s, N1s, and O1s data of the XPS analysis (S1, Supplementary data), the atomic percentage of nitrogen concentration is calculated to be 9.5at.%, close to that of 10.5at.% determined by the CHN elemental analysis. In Fig. 3e, three bands centered at 530.4 (19.9%), 531.8 (43.7%), and 535.2eV (36.5%) are separately attributed to oxygen components in the form of CO, COH/COC, and OCO [20,34]. The surface carboxyls of NPDs can be reflected by their ζ-potential plot (Fig. 3f) [13], where NPDs are negatively charged in water with pH 7. By varying the solution pH, there is only slight variation in ζ-potential value at pH ranging from 2 to 11. This result indicates a buffering function of NPDs due to the coexistence of amino/amide and carboxyl groups.

The N,O-functionalized surface improves the solubility of NPDs in water, offers options for various types of surface modification, and forms surface states with various energy levels. The surface states can serve as emission centers, which play a crucial role in the optical properties of CDs [14,26].

The morphology and particle size of the NPDs were examined with the SPM and TEM techniques (Fig. 4 ). Quasi-spherical and relatively uniform dots with depth distribution of 2–5.5nm are observed (Fig. 4, a–c). By counting 150 dots in Fig. 4d, the size distribution of NPDs is determined to be 2–9nm with mean diameter of 3.6±1.1nm (Fig. 4d, inset). This is in accordance with the result of depth histogram (Fig. 4c) obtained by SPM, due to the nearly monolayer of NPDs deposited on a mica. It should be pointed out that the size distribution of the dots can be tuned by varying the concentration of PBPs in reaction medium (Fig. S2), rendering an average particle size of 3.96, 2.60 and 2.03nm for NPD-28 (Fig. 4c), NPD-50 (Fig. S2a and c), and NPD-83 (Fig. S2b and d), respectively. This is attributed to the increase in KOH concentration with increasing PBP concentration in reaction medium, as mentioned before. In fact, PPBP nanodot aggregates insoluble in water can be obtained without KOH. The PPBP nanodots in water emit luminescence concentrated at 442nm (Fig. S3). As a result, we can conclude that the alkaline environment plays a crucial role in the formation of NPDs, as illustrated in Fig. 1. Fig. 4e shows clear lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.277, 0.239, and 0.168nm, corresponding separately to the d 200 of primitive cubic carbon with Pm3m space group [41], d 100 of graphite [7,42], and d 004 of graphitic carbon [7,42]. This result reveals the carbogenic characteristic of NPDs, in accordance with the Raman result (Fig. 2b).

The optical properties of the NPDs were examined by the UV–vis and PL techniques. Fig. 5 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra. It is found that there is no apparent characteristic peak in the absorption region with onset at ca. 700nm. The featureless absorption profiles have been observed in previous reports with respect to carbon dots [6,9], nitrogen-doped carbon dots [21], and oxidized graphene sheets [31]. Also, the absorption profiles are analogous to those of semiconductor dots [6]. The PL excitation (PLE) spectra (Fig. 5a, inset) recorded in the range of 250–450nm exhibits two peaks centered at 270 and 362nm. The former corresponds to the strong background absorption from π→π* transition, and the latter corresponds to the band hiding in the strong background absorption (Fig. 5a) [3,31]. Under excitation at 360nm, the NPD samples emit strong blue luminescence with wavelength concentrated around 470nm (Fig. S4a). The full-width at half-maximum of the emission bands is less than 104nm, suggesting a narrow particle size distribution of the NPD samples [6,15]. This is in concert with the SPM and TEM observations (Fig. 4).

In association with the UV–vis (Fig. 5a) and PLE spectra (Fig. 5a, inset), the small discrepancy between the NPD emission spectra (Fig. S4a) indicates a size-independent optical performance. This is attributed to the overlapping particle size distribution and the similar structure and surface functional groups with respect to the NPD samples (Fig. S5). The surface functional groups revealed by the FT-IR, 13C NMR, and XPS spectra (Fig. 3), such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, amino/amide, and ether linkage, form surface states lying in between the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of sp2-hybridized CC [26]. The energy levels of NPDs can be estimated from the cyclic voltammograms (Fig. S6) and the PLE spectrum (Fig. 5a, inset) [5], rendering the HOMO and LUMO levels of sp2-hybridized CC at −(7.96–8.38) and −(3.36–3.78)eV, respectively. Therefore, the energy levels of surface states are located at −(6.79–7.21) eV, which are at 1.17eV above the HOMO levels of NPDs (Fig. S7).

For most of carbogenic dots such as carbon dots [4,6,8,9], graphene quantum dots [26,31], carbon nitride quantum dots [32], nitrogen-doped carbon dots [3,22], and polymer dots [33], their PL emission spectra are universally dependent on the full range of excitation wavelength (λ ex ). In the present case, the peak intensity of the emission spectra increases with increasing λ ex yet the maximum peak position does not change in the λ ex range of 320–360nm (Fig. 5b), rendering a λ ex -independent emission behavior. However, the maximum peak position red-shifts from 469 to 567nm as the λ ex increases from 360 to 500nm (Fig. 5b, inset), indicating a λ ex -dependent emission behavior. The dual emission behavior of NPDs can be reflected by their photographs taken at different excitation wavelengths through a fluorescence microscope (Fig. S4b), and has been observed yet not explained in previous reports [4,5,17]. The λ ex -independent emission behavior has been observed on green-emission (517nm) carbon dots [16], blue-emission nitrogen-doped CDs (455 and 450nm) [18,21] and PEI-passivated graphene quantum dots (460nm) [27], with λ ex
≤400nm. One explanation is that this is due to the relatively uniform surface status of carbon dots because carbon dots with less complex surface status afford PL with unvaried energies at different excitations [16]. Another explanation is that this arises from the bandgap-like transitions related to amide chromophores residing on the CDs [18,21]. This is similar to the bandgap-like PL of molecular dyes carrying amide groups, which undergo direct singlet-to-triplet transitions and exhibit strong PL emissions at 455±15nm [43]. Third, the quantum-confinement mechanism involving isolated sp2-hybridized carbon clusters is responsible for the λ ex -independent emissions [27]. On the other hand, the λ ex -dependent emission behavior could be a result of varied fluorescence characteristics of nanodots with different sizes and different emissive centers [2,10,26]. Recently, a comprehensive study on the PL of carbon dots revealed that the fluorescence originates from charge recombination on surface emission centers, involving a strong coupling between the electronic transition and collective vibrational modes of the lattice structure [42]. In the present case, the crystalline NPDs with narrow size distribution (Fig. 4) and the coexistence of N,O-containing functional groups (Fig. 3) are responsible for the interesting dual emission behavior. Under excitations with λ ex ranging from 320 to 360nm, radiative recombinations related to both surface amide chromophores and crystalline sp2-hybridized CC domains overwhelms those related to the other functional groups (–OH, CO, –COOH), rendering a λ ex -independent emission behavior; and vice versa, tunable emissions occur under excitations with λ ex longer than 360nm.

As mentioned before, the coexistence of N,O-functional groups offers NPDs a buffer function (Fig. 3f). This is helpful for stabilizing the emissions of NPDs in a wide pH range. As expected, the PL intensity and the maximum emission peak position retain nearly constant as the solution pH varies from 2 to 11 (Fig. 5c), indicating a pH-insensitive PL property of NPDs. The decrease in PL intensity in strong acidic and/or strong alkaline solution is a common feature for CDs [4,5,8,31–33]. This is because heavily charged NPD surface inhibits charge recombination on surface emission centers [4]. Nevertheless, the pH-insensitive property ensures potential applications of NPDs in a wide pH range (pH 2–11). Moreover, the ionic strength of the medium has a minor effect on the emission behavior of NPDs (Fig. S8). With excitation at 360nm, the PL intensity decreases by 10.5% as the concentration of KCl varies from 0 to 2M. The ionic strength-resistant optical property offers NPDs a great potential for application in bio-related fields [3,33]. To explore the potential application in polymer film-based optoelectronic devices [11], the emission spectra of NPDs in organic solvents were also measured, as shown in Fig. S9. It is found that NPDs in methanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) also emit strong luminescence with maximum emission peak centered at 480, 488, and 506nm, respectively. This result indicates a solvent-dependent emission behavior similar to other carbon dots [8,32]. However, the emission peaks for NPDs in organic solvents are broadened and their symmetries are not as good as that in water. Indeed, the PL mechanism for CDs in organic solvents has not been well understood [8,32], rendering a scientific issue deserving further investigation.

The PL quantum yield (QY) is a very reliable measure of the suitability of luminescent materials because it determines the ratio between the radiative decay channel and the sum of radiative and nonradiant relaxation [44]. The QY of NPDs in water was determined by employing an absolute quantum yield measurement method [26,44]. As shown in Fig. S10a, the QY of NPDs is measured to be 3%, a value that is characteristic of carbon dots with unpassivated surface [2] and is higher than that of polymer dots in previous reports [33,34]. The QY of NPDs is not high, but this does not compromise their applications in bioimaging and metal-ion sensing as demonstrated in literature [33,34]. This is due to the robust photostability against photobleaching under 360-nm excitation for 1h (Fig. S11). Also, the NPD colloid (2mgmL−1) is stable during the storage at room temperature for ca. 6 months. Furthermore, there is only slight decrease in PL intensity of NPDs after six-month storage under excitation at 360nm (Fig. S12). Fig. 5d shows the time-resolved emission spectrum of NPDs in aqueous solution with excitation at 362nm. The luminescence time profile matches well with biexponential fitting functions, yielding lifetimes of 1.96 and 7.14ns. The average lifetime is 4.21ns. This suggests several competing deactivation pathways within the dots. The short lifetime is associated with the population of trap states while the long lifetime is related to the intrinsic and trap-mediated luminescence processes [13].

The emission behavior of CDs is influenced by their surface chemistry [17,30]. As demonstrated in literature [17,30], partial carboxyls and carbonyls on the surface of carbon dots can be changed into hydroxyls through NaBH4 reduction, yielding either an enhanced QY and blue-shifted emissions with increased intensity [17] or a reduced QY and unshifted emissions with decreased intensity [30]. To explore the effect of surface chemistry on the emission behavior of NPDs, we performed experiments on reducing NPDs in NaBH4 aqueous solutions with different concentrations. After surface reduction, the maximum emission peak blue-shifts to 452nm from 470nm under λ ex at 360nm, and the PL intensity dramatically increases (Fig. 5e). As the λ ex increases from 320 to 500nm (Fig. 5f), the PL intensity gradually decreases and the maximum emission peak red-shifts to 542nm from 428nm, presenting a λ ex -dependent emission behavior of the reduced NPDs (r-NPDs). Also, the QY of r-NPDs is greatly improved to be 24% (Fig. S10b) whereas the average lifetime remains nearly constant (4.3ns). This is in concert with the overlapping emission decay curves of NPDs and r-NPDs (Fig. 5d). The surface chemistry-dependent emission behavior of NPDs is consistent with the result in previous reports [17,26], where surface hydroxyls function as a passivation layer beneficial for effective luminescence emissions. Indeed, the concentration of hydroxyls is greatly increased whereas the concentration of carboxyls is dramatically decreased after surface reduction (Table S2), according to the high-resolution C 1s and O 1s spectra of NPDs and r-NPDs (Fig. S13).

On the basis of the above analysis, NPDs have been obtained through a microwave-assisted ethanolthermal synthesis at 80°C. The NPDs are featured with high nitrogen content, abundant N,O-containing functional groups, a high degree of crystallinity, a tunable size distribution, and a narrow blue emission at ca. 470nm with high photostability. The PL emission behavior of NPDs exhibits a dual λ ex -independent and λ ex -dependent property before and after λ ex at 360nm. Also, NPDs exhibit pH-independent (pH 2–11), ionic strength-resistent, and solvent-dependent emissions. After surface reduction, the maximum emission peak of r-NPDs blue-shifts and the QY is greatly enhanced, compared with the original NPDs. The as-obtained NPDs present a promising candidate for applications in bioimaging, drug delivery, photocatalysis, energy storage/conversion, and chemical sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of NPDs by ACs
ce:section.ce:para: Table S3 provides the results of fluorescence-quenching of NPDs by 20 ACs. Unlike the result in previous report [12], the quenching ability of ACs toward NPDs is not related to their capability of electron-donating or electron-withdrawing, because the emissions of NPDs cannot be apparently suppressed by such electron donors (toluene, phenol, aniline, hydroquinone, p-phenylenediamine, N,N-diethylaniline, and dopamine) and electron acceptors (benzaldehyde, benzoic acid, nitrobenzene, 4-nitrobenzaldyhyde, 4-nitrobenzoic acid, 4-nitrobenzene, and 2,4-dinitrobenzene), under excitation at 360nm (Table S3). On the contrary, nitrophenol and nitroaniline compounds especially 2,4,6-trinitrophenol cause apparent fluorescence quenching of NPDs (Table S3). Aside from toluene, all the other ACs contain more than one polar head groups that can combine with N,O-functionalized NPDs through noncovalent interactions. As a result, fluorescence quenching caused by π–π stacking and noncovalent interactions between NPDs and ACs can be excluded [22,29].

As mentioned before, it is requisite to determine the HOMO and LUMO levels of quenchers before assigning the quenching mechanism of NPDs [5,30]. Through theoretical calculations, the HOMO and LUMO levels of 20 ACs are obtained, as illustrated in Fig. 6 . It is surprising to find that only the LUMO level of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol is below those of NPDs, a situation that satisfies the prerequisite of charge transfer under excitation at 360nm. Also, fluorescence quenching of NPDs by the tested 20 ACs through the FRET route cannot take place because their energy level differences (ΔE) are higher than that of 3.45eV under excitation at 360nm (Table S4).

To get insight into the fluorescence quenching of NPDs by ACs, the UV–vis absorbance spectra with respect to 20 ACs and NPDs in solution were measured (Fig. S14). There are relatively strong absorbance at 360nm in spectra for compounds that can induce apparent quenching of NPDs (Fig. S14a), whereas the compounds that cannot induce quenching of NPDs under excitation at 360nm exhibit nearly no absorbance at 360nm (Fig. S14b). Also, there is nearly no difference between the spectra of individual compound and compound with NPDs (Fig. S14a), making it impossible for fluorescence quenching of NPDs through the formation of Meisenheimer complex [15]. On the basis of the above analysis, we infer that the fluorescence quenching of NPDs is mainly related to the UV–vis absorbance spectra of ACs. As an inference, any aromatic compound can cause fluorescence quenching of NPDs when λ ex is set at or near the maximum absorption of the compound. To prove this inference, fluorescence quenching tests were performed on NPDs in aqueous solutions containing ACs that cannot induce quenching of NPDs with λ ex at 360nm, such as nitrobenzene, 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde, 4-nitrobenzoic acid, 4-nitrotoluene, and 2,4-dinitrotoluene. As expected, the emissions of NPDs can be dramatically suppressed by these compounds under excitation at their own maximum absorption wavelengths (Table S5). It should be pointed out that ACs, which are photoluminescent under excitation at their maximum absorption wavelengths, exhibit emissions of their own rather than that of NPDs (data not shown). In this research, these luminescent compounds comprise phenol, aniline, hydroquinone, p-phenylenediamine, N,N-diethylaniline, and dopamine. Moreover, the plots of quenching degree as a function of λ ex (Fig. 7 ) resemble the UV–vis absorbance spectra (Fig. 7, inset), taking 4-nitrophenol and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol as examples.

Through the theoretical calculations and instrumental analysis, we conclude that non-photochemical quenching rather than the FRET and charge transfer dominates the fluorescence quenching of NPDs by ACs. This is attributed to the excitation dissipation resulting from light absorption by ACs, causing a reduced luminous flux exciting NPDs. On the other hand, charge transfer contributes further to the quenching of NPDs by 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, whose LUMO level matches with those of NPDs. This is reflected by the result (Table S2) that a relatively smaller mole of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (43μmol) offers a relatively higher degree of quenching (60.7%). Nevertheless, fluorescence quenching contributed from charge transfer is inferior to that from light absorption by ACs. This result is based on (i) the degree of quenching of NPDs by 2,4,6-trinitrophenol is highly dependent on λ ex (Fig. 7); (ii) some compounds with ΔE higher than 4.6eV exhibit effective quenching to NPDs (Table S4), under excitation at the maximum absorption wavelengths of the compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective fluorescent detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol with NPDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Since the fluorescence emissions of NPDs are more sensitive to 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, fluorescent detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol with NPDs was investigated. Fig. 8 a shows the emission spectra of NPDs in aqueous solutions containing different concentrations of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol under excitation at 360nm. It is apparent that the degree of quenching is gradually increases as the quencher concentration increases, accompanying with a slight red-shift of the maximum emission peak to 485nm. Also, the fluorescence quenching of NPDs with 2,4,6-trinitrophenol follows the Stern–Volmer equation [22,28,29],
I 0/I =1+ K SV C
where I 0 and I represent the emission intensity without and with quencher, respectively; K SV is the Stern–Volmer fluorescence quenching constant, which is a measure of the efficiency of quenching by quencher; and C is the quencher concentration. Linear fitting of the plot of I 0/I versus C produces a K SV of 0.036μM−1 (Fig. 8a, inset). The detection limit of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol with NPDs is calculated to be 4μM (∼0.92ppm) at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, comparable to the result for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection with amine-capped carbon dots [22]. In addition, fluorescence quenching of NPDs instantly occurs upon addition of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol and remains nearly constant with prolonging duration time (Fig. S15). This indicates a rapid fluorescent response and rules out the formation of Meisenheimer complex [15]. The rapid fluorescent response is beneficial for the NPD-based chemical sensors. More significantly, NPDs show high sensitivity to 2,4,6-trinitrophenol even if the solution contains six other ACs such as aniline, nitrobenzene, 4-nitrobenzaldehyde, 4-nitrotoluene, dopamine, and N,N-diethylaniline, which cannot induce fluorescence quenching of NPDs under excitation at 360nm (Fig. 8b). This result ensures effective fluorescent detection of trace 2,4,6-trinitrophenol through NPD-based sensors.

In summary, nitrogen-containing carbogenic polymer dots have been acquired through a microwave-assisted ethanolthermal synthesis at a low temperature of 80°C. The scientific significance of this research lies in (1) a simple approach is offered for low-temperature synthesis of polymer dots with relatively high nitrogen concentration and high yield; (2) a dual λ ex -independent and λ ex -dependent emission behavior of the polymer dots under excitation before and after 360nm is explained; (3) the absolute quantum yield of the polymer dots can be greatly improved to be 24% through simple surface reduction; (4) insight into the fluorescence quenching of the polymer dots by aromatic compounds is provided; and (5) the polymer dots show highly selective sensitivity to 2,4,6-trinitrophenol in aqueous solution. The as-obtained nitrogen-containing polymer dots have a great potential in chemical sensing, bioimaging, photocatalysis, and energy storage/conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 51272143 and 51472278.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.04.047.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data involve additional data regarding characterization, optical properties, and fluorescence quenching of NPDs, and theoretical calculation results with respect to the energy levels of 20 aromatic compounds.
The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhenzhen Guo, postgraduate in Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology, Jinan 250353, People’s Republic of China.

Ligang Gai, leading professor in Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology, Jinan 250353, People’s Republic of China. He received his Ph.D in 2006 from Shandong University, Jinan 250100, People’s Republic of China.

Jianhua Zhou, professor in Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology, Jinan 250353, People’s Republic of China.

Haihui Jiang, professor in Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology, Jinan 250353, People’s Republic of China.

Yan Tian, associate professor in Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology, Jinan 250353, People’s Republic of China.
